expanded_description: An introduction to Class Agnatha—the jawless vertebrates, including lampreys and hagfishes. This guide explains what makes these animals unique, how they survive without jaws, and why they are important for understanding vertebrate evolution.

core_concept.principle: Agnathans are jawless vertebrates that lack paired fins and have a cartilaginous skeleton with a persistent notochord instead of a backbone. Lampreys and hagfishes are the only living jawless vertebrates, and they provide clues about what early vertebrates looked like before jaws evolved.
core_concept.explanation:
Class Agnatha includes about 120 species of jawless vertebrates. The two main groups are lampreys (~40 species) and hagfishes (~80 species). These animals are the only living vertebrates that never evolved jaws. Instead, they have special adaptations for feeding without them.

Key features of agnathans:
• No jaws—lampreys have a circular mouth with teeth for attaching to fish; hagfishes have a slit-like mouth with tooth plates for scraping flesh.
• No paired fins—they swim by wiggling their bodies like eels.
• No backbone—they have a flexible rod called a notochord for support instead of vertebrae.
• Cartilaginous skeleton—their skeletons are made of cartilage, not bone.
• Multiple gill openings—instead of one gill cover like most fish, they have 7 or more separate gill holes.

Lampreys have a complex life cycle. Young lampreys (called ammocoetes) live in freshwater streams, filter-feeding like tiny whales. After 3-7 years, they transform into adults. Some lampreys attach to fish and suck their blood; others don't feed at all as adults and die soon after spawning. Hagfishes live only in the ocean and are scavengers. They feed on dead fish and whales that fall to the seafloor.

Agnathans are important because they are living windows into the past. They show us what the earliest vertebrates looked like—before jaws, before paired fins, and before backbones. By studying them, we learn how vertebrates evolved.

image.alt: Lamprey (eel-like with round mouth) and hagfish (eel-like with slime pores). Diagram showing jawless mouth and gill openings.
image.caption: Jawless vertebrates: Lampreys (parasitic, anadromous) and hagfishes (scavengers, marine). Both lack jaws, paired fins, and a true backbone.

sections.introduction:
When you think of vertebrates, you probably think of fish with jaws, frogs, birds, or mammals. But the earliest vertebrates had no jaws at all. They were simple, eel-like animals that filter-fed or scavenged for food. Their descendants still live today—they are the lampreys and hagfishes.

Lampreys and hagfishes are the only living jawless vertebrates. They are not "primitive" in the sense of being unchanged; they have been evolving for over 500 million years, just like all other animals. But they have kept features that most other vertebrates lost, making them valuable for understanding our own evolutionary history.

What makes an agnathan?
• No jaws—they feed without biting
• No paired fins—they swim by wiggling
• No backbone—they have a flexible notochord instead
• Multiple gill openings—7 or more pairs
• Cartilaginous skeleton—made of cartilage, not bone

In this section, we'll explore how lampreys and hagfishes live, how they survive without jaws, and what they tell us about the origins of vertebrates.

introduction_image.alt: Simple evolutionary tree: Agnathans (lampreys + hagfishes) branch off before jawed vertebrates.
introduction_image.caption: Evolutionary tree: Lampreys and hagfishes are the oldest living vertebrate lineages. They branched off before jaws evolved.

key_concept.definition: Agnathans are jawless vertebrates that lack paired fins and have a notochord instead of a backbone. Lampreys and hagfishes are the only living representatives of this ancient group.
key_concept.context:
Both lampreys and hagfishes have survived for over 500 million years, long before dinosaurs and even before fish with jaws. They have adapted to their environments in unique ways. Lampreys use a suction cup mouth to attach to fish and rasp their flesh. Hagfishes produce massive amounts of slime to defend themselves and can tie their bodies into knots to escape predators or tear food.

Why study agnathans? Because they are living fossils. Their bodies reveal what the earliest vertebrates looked like. By comparing them to jawed fish, we can see what changed when jaws evolved and what stayed the same.

image.alt: Lamprey and hagfish with labels: jawless mouth, notochord, gill openings, no paired fins.
image.caption: Agnathan features: No jaws, no paired fins, notochord instead of backbone, multiple gill openings.

sections.key_concept: Lampreys and hagfishes have different ways of surviving without jaws. Lampreys attach to fish and suck blood; hagfishes scavenge dead animals and defend themselves with slime. Both have flexible bodies supported by a notochord.

classifications.value: Understanding lamprey feeding, life cycle, and adaptations
characteristics: Mouth: Circular oral disc with rings of sharp, keratinous teeth. Acts like a suction cup to attach to fish.
Feeding: Parasitic species attach to fish, use a rasping tongue to scrape through skin, and suck blood and body fluids. Some species don't feed as adults.
Skeleton: Notochord (flexible rod) supported by small cartilage pieces (arcualia). No vertebrae.
Life cycle: Ammocoete larvae (3-7 years, filter-feed in freshwater) → metamorphosis → adult (migrates to sea/lake to feed) → spawns and dies.
Location: Freshwater and marine; some species migrate between both (anadromous).
classifications.behavior:
Lampreys have a two-stage life cycle. Young lampreys (ammocoetes) are blind filter-feeders. They bury in mud and suck in water, trapping tiny food particles on their gill slits. They look a lot like lancelets—small, simple filter-feeders that are not even vertebrates. This suggests that early vertebrates may have looked like this too.

After several years, lampreys undergo metamorphosis. Their eyes develop, their mouth changes into a sucking disc with teeth, and they transform into adults. Some lampreys become parasites, attaching to fish with their oral disc and using their tongue to rasp through scales and skin. They release chemicals that keep the fish's blood from clotting so they can feed for hours. Other lampreys do not feed at all as adults—they live off energy stored as larvae, spawn, and die.

Lampreys swim by undulating their whole body. Without paired fins, they can't steer as well as fish, but their flexible body lets them maneuver in tight spaces. Their notochord provides support while still allowing them to bend.

examples.explanation:
Lamprey life cycle in stages:
(1) Ammocoete larva: Lives in freshwater streams for 3-7 years. Filters food from water. Blind and toothless.
(2) Metamorphosis: Triggered by thyroid hormones. Eyes develop, mouth transforms into oral disc with teeth.
(3) Adult: Migrates to sea or lake. Parasitic species attach to fish and feed. Non-parasitic species do not feed.
(4) Spawning: Returns to freshwater streams, builds a nest (redd), spawns, and dies.

Why this matters: The ammocoete stage looks like a lancelet—a simple invertebrate chordate. This suggests that early vertebrates may have started as filter-feeders before evolving jaws and becoming predators.

image.alt: Lamprey life cycle: ammocoete larva → metamorphosis → adult → spawning and death.
image.caption: Lamprey life cycle: Filter-feeding larva (3-7 years) → metamorphosis → parasitic adult → spawn and die.

classifications.value: Understanding hagfish feeding, defense, and deep-sea adaptations
characteristics: Mouth: Terminal slit with tooth plates (not a circular disc). Barbels around mouth for sensing food.
Feeding: Scavenger. Enters dead or dying animals through mouth or anus, eats from inside out using tooth plates.
Defense: Slime glands along body release huge amounts of slime that clog predators' gills. One hagfish can turn a bucket of water into slime in seconds.
Skeleton: Only notochord—no vertebrae, no cartilage pieces. Extremely flexible.
Location: Marine only (deep sea). Cannot survive in freshwater.
classifications.behavior:
Hagfishes are deep-sea scavengers. They spend most of their time looking for dead animals that sink to the ocean floor. When they find a carcass, they use their tooth plates to rasp away flesh. They can even enter the carcass through the mouth or anus to eat from the inside out.

Hagfishes have an incredible defense: slime. Along their body are 100-200 slime glands. When threatened, they release slime that expands instantly in seawater. A single hagfish can turn a bucket of water into slime in seconds. The slime clogs the gills of predators like sharks, forcing them to retreat. Hagfishes can also tie their bodies into knots. They slide the knot from head to tail to scrape off slime or to gain leverage when tearing flesh.

Hagfishes have a notochord but no vertebrae at all. Their body is so flexible they can tie themselves in knots. They also have very simple eyes—just light-sensitive spots under the skin. In the dark deep sea, they rely on smell and electroreception to find food.

examples.explanation:
How hagfish slime works:
(1) Slime glands contain two types of cells: thread cells (long protein fibers) and mucin cells (slime-making proteins).
(2) When a predator bites or threatens the hagfish, the glands contract and release their contents.
(3) In seawater, the threads instantly unravel and the mucin expands, creating a massive volume of sticky, gel-like slime.
(4) The slime clogs the predator's gills, causing it to retreat.
(5) The hagfish removes slime from its own body by tying a knot and sliding it off.

Why this matters: Hagfish slime is one of the most effective defenses in the animal kingdom. It's so effective that hagfishes have few natural predators. Scientists are studying hagfish slime fibers to develop new materials—they are stronger than spider silk!

image.alt: Hagfish releasing slime; slime glands; knotting behavior to remove slime.
image.caption: Hagfish defense: Slime glands release threads that expand in seawater, clogging predator gills. Knotting removes slime from its own body.

principles.principle: Living Agnathans Are Models for Understanding Early Vertebrates
principles.statement: Lampreys and hagfishes have kept features that other vertebrates lost. By studying them, we can understand what the earliest vertebrates were like.
principles.application:
This helps us understand:
• What the first vertebrates looked like (eel-like, jawless, filter-feeding or scavenging)
• How vertebrates lived before they evolved jaws
• Why some features (like a backbone) evolved after others

**Everyday connection**: If you want to know what a Model T Ford looked like, you can look at one in a museum. Lampreys and hagfishes are like living museum specimens—they show us what early vertebrates looked like, even though they are modern animals.
principles.example: The ammocoete larva of lampreys looks remarkably like lancelets (invertebrate chordates). Both are filter-feeders with a notochord and gill slits. This suggests that the earliest vertebrates were probably small, filter-feeding animals similar to ammocoetes. Only later did vertebrates evolve jaws and become predators.
principles.analogy: Lampreys and hagfishes are like living time capsules. They preserve features from our distant past—features that other vertebrates changed or lost. Studying them is like opening a window into vertebrate evolution.

image.alt: Comparison of lamprey ammocoete and lancelet (invertebrate chordate) showing similarities.
image.caption: Lamprey ammocoete (larva) and lancelet (invertebrate chordate) look similar. Both are filter-feeders with a notochord. This suggests early vertebrates were filter-feeders.

principles.principle: Surviving Without Jaws Requires Special Adaptations
principles.statement: Lampreys and hagfishes cannot bite. They have evolved other ways to feed: lampreys attach and rasp; hagfishes scavenge and use their body as a tool.
principles.application:
This shows that:
• Evolution can find different solutions to the same problem (how to get food without jaws)
• Behavior can compensate for missing structures (knotting, suction feeding)
• Being "primitive" doesn't mean being unsuccessful—both groups have thrived for over 500 million years

**Everyday connection**: Imagine trying to eat without using your jaws. You'd have to suck your food, or scrape it, or find a way to get inside it. That's what lampreys and hagfishes do—they've found creative solutions to the challenge of jawless living.
principles.example: Lampreys use suction and a rasping tongue to feed on live fish. Hagfishes use their flexible bodies to tie knots, creating leverage to tear flesh from carcasses. Both methods work, even though neither group has jaws. This shows that there is more than one way to be a successful vertebrate.
principles.analogy: Jawless feeding is like being a mechanic without a wrench. You can still fix the car—you just have to use pliers, or your hands, or find another way. Lampreys and hagfishes are experts at finding other ways to get the job done.

image.alt: Lamprey attached to fish (rasping) and hagfish inside carcass (scavenging).
image.caption: Feeding without jaws: Lampreys (attach and rasp), Hagfishes (scavenge and use knotting). Both solutions evolved independently.

principles.principle: Agnathans Show That Evolution Doesn't Always Add Features
principles.statement: Sometimes evolution removes features. Hagfishes lack features that lampreys have (like image-forming eyes and vertebrae). This doesn't mean hagfishes are more "primitive"—it means they adapted to their environment by losing what they didn't need.
principles.application:
This teaches us that:
• Evolution can involve loss as well as gain
• "Primitive" appearance can be the result of adaptation, not ancestry
• We can't assume that simpler animals are closer to the ancestor

**Everyday connection**: If you live in a cave, you don't need eyes. Over time, cave animals often lose their eyes. That doesn't mean they are more primitive—it means they adapted to their environment. Hagfishes live in the dark deep sea, so they lost their functional eyes. Lampreys live in brighter waters, so they kept theirs.
principles.example: Hagfishes have no vertebrae and very simple eyes. Lampreys have simple vertebrae (arcualia) and image-forming eyes. For a long time, scientists thought hagfishes branched off first because they seemed more "primitive." But DNA evidence shows that hagfishes and lampreys are equally old—hagfishes lost their vertebrae and eyes because they adapted to the deep sea.
principles.analogy: Evolution is like remodeling a house. Sometimes you add a room. Sometimes you tear one down. Lampreys kept the "rooms" they needed; hagfishes removed the "rooms" they didn't need. Both houses are equally old—they just have different features.

image.alt: Lamprey (eyes, simple vertebrae) vs. hagfish (no eyes, no vertebrae).
image.caption: Trait loss: Hagfishes lost features (eyes, vertebrae) that lampreys kept. This is adaptation to deep-sea life, not primitiveness.

components.definition: Agnathans have jawless mouths, notochord-based skeletons, multiple gill openings, and specialized feeding structures (oral disc in lampreys, slime glands in hagfishes).
components.equation: Agnathan survival = Behavioral adaptation × Physiological specialization
components.interpretation: Lampreys and hagfishes succeed not because they have complex skeletons or jaws, but because they have evolved clever behaviors (knotting, suction feeding) and special chemicals (anticoagulants, slime) that help them survive in their niches.

image.alt: Lamprey: oral disc, notochord, gill openings. Hagfish: slime glands, tooth plates, barbels.
image.caption: Agnathan features: Lampreys (oral disc, notochord, multiple gill openings); Hagfishes (slime glands, tooth plates, barbels).

categories.type: Lamprey Life Cycle: Filter-Feeder to Parasite
categories.description: Lampreys transform from filter-feeding larvae to parasitic adults—a dramatic change controlled by hormones.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Ammocoete larva: Lives in freshwater streams for 3-7 years. Filters food from water. Blind, no teeth, no oral disc. Burrows in mud. Looks like a lancelet (invertebrate chordate).

Metamorphosis: Triggered by thyroid hormone (same hormone that controls frog metamorphosis!). Eyes develop, mouth transforms into oral disc with teeth, gut changes for blood-feeding.

Adult: Migrates to sea or lake. Parasitic species attach to fish and feed. Non-parasitic species do not feed as adults.

Spawning: Returns to freshwater, builds nest, spawns, dies.

examples.explanation:
Lamprey metamorphosis is controlled by the same hormone that triggers metamorphosis in frogs. This shows that:
(1) Thyroid hormone is an ancient vertebrate hormone—it existed long before frogs.
(2) Different vertebrates use the same hormone to control different life stage transitions.
(3) Lampreys and frogs inherited this hormone system from a common ancestor over 400 million years ago.

Why this matters: Studying lamprey metamorphosis helps us understand human thyroid disorders. The same hormone controls your metabolism—and lampreys show us how it works.

image.alt: Lamprey metamorphosis triggered by thyroid hormone; comparison with frog metamorphosis.
image.caption: Thyroid hormone controls metamorphosis in both lampreys and frogs—an ancient vertebrate hormone system conserved for 400+ million years.

categories.description: Hagfish slime is one of the most effective defenses in the animal kingdom. It's made of protein fibers that expand in seawater to clog predator gills.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Slime glands: 100-200 glands along the body. Each gland contains two types of cells: thread cells (long protein fibers) and mucin cells (slime-making proteins).

Release: When threatened, the glands contract and release their contents into seawater.

Expansion: The threads instantly unravel and the mucin expands, creating a massive volume of sticky slime—up to 20 liters from one hagfish!

Defense: The slime clogs the gills of predators (especially sharks), forcing them to retreat.

Cleaning: The hagfish ties its body into a knot and slides the knot from head to tail, scraping off the slime.

examples.process: Hagfish Slime as a Model Material
examples.explanation:
Hagfish slime fibers are incredibly strong—stronger than spider silk, which is already stronger than steel! Scientists are studying these fibers to create new materials:
• Synthetic slime fibers for textiles (bulletproof vests, lightweight ropes)
• Biodegradable plastics (the fibers break down naturally)
• Medical sutures (strong and biocompatible)

Why this matters: A 500-million-year-old animal is teaching us how to make better materials. Nature often has solutions that engineers haven't thought of yet.

image.alt: Hagfish slime threads; potential applications: sutures, ropes, biodegradable plastics.
image.caption: Hagfish slime fibers are stronger than spider silk. Scientists study them to develop new materials—from medical sutures to bulletproof vests.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Comparing Lampreys and Hagfishes

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Two Jawless Vertebrates, Two Lifestyles
worked_examples.problem:
Compare a sea lamprey (Petromyzon marinus) and a Pacific hagfish (Eptatretus stoutii).
(1) How does each feed without jaws?
(2) How does their skeleton support their lifestyle?
(3) Why do they have different life cycles?
(4) Why are both still around after 500 million years?

step_by_step.calculation: Sea lamprey: Uses oral disc to attach to fish. Rasping tongue with teeth scrapes through skin. Anticoagulant keeps blood flowing. Pacific hagfish: Scavenges dead animals. Tooth plates rasp flesh. Uses knotting to gain leverage. Can enter carcasses through mouth or anus.

Sea lamprey: Notochord with small cartilage pieces (arcualia). Flexible body for undulatory swimming and attaching to fish. Pacific hagfish: Notochord only (no cartilage pieces). Extremely flexible—can tie into knots. Perfect for squeezing into carcasses and escaping predators.

Sea lamprey: Complex life cycle (larva → metamorphosis → adult). Allows use of both freshwater (larva) and marine (adult) resources. Pacific hagfish: Direct development (hatches as miniature adult). Stable deep-sea environment doesn't require complex life cycle.

Both occupy niches where jawed vertebrates are less competitive. Lampreys exploit parasitic lifestyle; hagfishes dominate deep-sea scavenging. Their special adaptations (oral disc, slime) give them advantages in these niches.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Lamprey: oral disc suction + rasping tongue; Hagfish: tooth plates + knotting. (2) Lamprey: notochord + arcualia for flexibility and attachment; Hagfish: notochord only for extreme flexibility. (3) Lamprey complex life cycle exploits freshwater and marine; Hagfish simple life cycle suits stable deep sea. (4) Both occupy niches where they have advantages; their special adaptations (slime, oral disc) help them compete.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lampreys and hagfishes show that jawless vertebrates can thrive by specializing in niches where jaws aren't an advantage. Their success demonstrates that there are many ways to be a vertebrate.

sections.introduction: Lampreys undergo one of the most dramatic life cycle transformations in the animal kingdom—from filter-feeding larva to parasitic adult.

process_flow.description: Young lamprey filters food from freshwater streams for 3-7 years.
process_flow.mechanism: Burrows in mud; oral hood creates current; food trapped on gill slits; buried in sediment.

image.alt: Ammocoete larva burrowed in mud, filter-feeding.
image.caption: Step 1: Ammocoete larva (3-7 years). Filter-feeds in freshwater streams. Resembles lancelet—shows what early vertebrates may have looked like.

process_flow.description: Thyroid hormone triggers transformation into adult form.
process_flow.mechanism: Eyes develop; oral disc forms; gut changes; gonads mature. Takes several weeks.
process_flow.result: Juvenile adult with eyes, oral disc, teeth

image.alt: Lamprey transforming: eyes developing, oral disc forming.
image.caption: Step 2: Metamorphosis. Thyroid hormone triggers transformation. Eyes develop, oral disc forms, gut changes.

process_flow.description: Migrates to sea/lake; attaches to fish and feeds.
process_flow.mechanism: Oral disc attaches; rasping tongue scrapes tissue; anticoagulant prevents clotting; feeds for hours-days.
process_flow.result: Parasitic feeding (or non-feeding in some species)

image.caption: Step 3: Adult feeding. Attaches to fish with oral disc, rasping tongue, anticoagulant saliva. Feeds for hours to days.

process_flow.description: Returns to freshwater, spawns, and dies.
process_flow.mechanism: Builds nest (redd) in gravel; mates; eggs hatch; adults die (semelparity).
process_flow.result: Life cycle complete; new generation begins

image.caption: Step 4: Spawning. Returns to freshwater, builds nest, spawns, dies. Cycle repeats.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about how DNA evidence and fossils help us understand agnathan evolution.

process_types.energy_profile: Comparing DNA sequences helps scientists figure out which animals are most closely related.
process_types.direction: Before DNA: Scientists thought hagfishes were the most primitive vertebrates because they seemed simplest. After DNA: Lampreys and hagfishes are equally old; hagfishes lost features (eyes, vertebrae) because they adapted to deep sea.
process_types.function: DNA helps us understand evolutionary relationships that appearances alone can hide.
goals: Understand that DNA can change what we thought we knew
Learn that simpler doesn't always mean more primitive
Appreciate that trait loss is common in evolution

primary_example.explanation:
For many years, scientists thought hagfishes were the most primitive vertebrates. They had no vertebrae, simple eyes, and seemed less complex than lampreys. But DNA told a different story.

When scientists sequenced DNA from lampreys and hagfishes, they found that the two groups share many genetic features that jawed vertebrates lack. This means lampreys and hagfishes are each other's closest relatives—they form a group called Cyclostomata.

If hagfishes were more primitive, they would share fewer genes with lampreys. Instead, they share many. This means that hagfishes are not primitive—they are highly specialized. They lost their eyes and vertebrae because they live in the dark deep sea, not because they never had them.

Why this matters: DNA evidence can overturn old ideas. Appearances can be deceiving. Hagfishes look simple, but their DNA tells us they are just as evolved as any other vertebrate—they just adapted differently.

image.alt: DNA tree: lampreys + hagfishes as sister group (Cyclostomata).
image.caption: DNA evidence: Lampreys and hagfishes are sister groups (Cyclostomata). Hagfishes lost features (eyes, vertebrae) due to deep-sea adaptation, not primitiveness.

image.alt: Lamprey (eyes) and hagfish (no eyes); both evolved from eyed ancestor.
image.caption: Trait loss: Both lampreys and hagfishes evolved from an ancestor with eyes. Hagfishes lost their eyes because they live in the dark deep sea.

process_types.energy_profile: Fossils show that jawless vertebrates were once much more diverse than they are today. Many had bony armor—something living agnathans lack.
process_types.direction: Cambrian: First chordates → Ordovician-Silurian: Ostracoderms (armored agnathans) dominate → Devonian: Jawed fish appear, ostracoderms decline → Present: Only lampreys and hagfishes remain
process_types.function: Fossils show us the diversity of agnathans in the past and help us understand why some groups survived while others died out.
goals: Learn that agnathans were once much more diverse
Understand that bony armor evolved before jaws
Appreciate that extinction is common in evolution

primary_example.explanation:
Ostracoderms were jawless fish that lived 500-360 million years ago. They had bony armor covering their heads and front bodies—something no living agnathan has. Some were bottom-dwellers; some swam in open water. They had no jaws, but they had paired fins (unlike lampreys and hagfishes).

Why did they go extinct? When jawed fish evolved, they could bite and hunt more efficiently. Ostracoderms couldn't compete. Only a few groups—the ancestors of lampreys and hagfishes—survived by moving into niches where jawed fish were less successful.

Why this matters: Ostracoderms show that agnathans were once the dominant vertebrates. Their decline was not because they were "primitive"—it was because jawed fish were better at being fish. Lampreys and hagfishes survived by becoming specialists (parasites and deep-sea scavengers).

image.alt: Ostracoderm fossil reconstruction showing bony armor.
image.caption: Ostracoderms: Armored jawless fish that dominated before jawed fish evolved. They had bony armor and paired fins—features lost in living agnathans.

image.alt: Timeline: Ordovician-Silurian (ostracoderms dominate) → Devonian (jawed fish rise) → Present (only lampreys and hagfishes).
image.caption: Agnathan timeline: Ostracoderms (armored) dominated before jawed fish. When jaws evolved, ostracoderms declined. Lampreys and hagfishes survived by specializing.

sections.relationship: Lampreys and hagfishes are the only living jawless vertebrates. They have survived for over 500 million years by adapting to niches where jawed vertebrates are less competitive. Lampreys became parasites, using suction and rasping to feed on live fish. Hagfishes became deep-sea scavengers, using slime for defense and knotting for leverage. Their skeletons are flexible (notochord instead of backbone), and they have no paired fins. DNA evidence shows they are each other's closest relatives (Cyclostomata) and that hagfishes lost features (eyes, vertebrae) due to deep-sea adaptation, not primitiveness. Fossils show that jawless vertebrates were once much more diverse, with bony armor and paired fins. Their decline coincided with the rise of jawed fish. Studying agnathans helps us understand what early vertebrates were like and how evolution works—sometimes losing features, sometimes gaining them, always adapting.

summary:
Class Agnatha includes the only living jawless vertebrates: lampreys and hagfishes. Both lack jaws, paired fins, and a true backbone (they have a notochord instead). They have cartilaginous skeletons and multiple gill openings.

Lampreys have a complex life cycle: filter-feeding larvae (ammocoetes) live in freshwater for 3-7 years, then metamorphose into adults. Some lampreys attach to fish and feed on blood; others don't feed as adults. Lampreys swim by undulating their bodies and use their oral disc (suction cup) to attach to hosts.

Hagfishes are marine scavengers. They feed on dead animals that sink to the ocean floor. They have slime glands that release massive amounts of slime to defend against predators. They can tie their bodies into knots to scrape off slime or to gain leverage when tearing flesh.

Agnathans are important for understanding vertebrate evolution. They show what early vertebrates looked like—before jaws, before paired fins, before backbones. DNA evidence reveals that lampreys and hagfishes are each other's closest relatives (Cyclostomata) and that hagfishes lost features (eyes, vertebrae) due to deep-sea adaptation, not primitiveness. Fossils show that jawless vertebrates were once much more diverse, with bony armor and paired fins, but most went extinct when jawed fish evolved.

Despite being "primitive" in some ways, lampreys and hagfishes are highly successful in their niches. They have survived for over 500 million years by evolving specialized adaptations: lampreys became parasites; hagfishes became slime-producing scavengers. They are living windows into our distant evolutionary past.
